meta.title: Global Mobility & Citizenship Blocs (Supranational Flag Paths)
meta.disclaimer: Informational only, not legal advice. Rules change frequently; verify with an immigration lawyer before acting.

tier_legend.TR: What a temporary residence permit unlocks

excluded.name: Singapore-Malaysia
excluded.reason: Work-pass facilitation and a border CIQ arrangement only; no mutual settlement rights.

excluded.name: SADC Free Movement Protocol
excluded.reason: Signed 2005 but never ratified by the required two-thirds of members; not in force.

excluded.name: COMESA Free Movement Protocol
excluded.reason: Adopted 1998; only Burundi has ratified; not in force.

excluded.name: MSG Skills Movement Scheme (Melanesia)
excluded.reason: Temporary skilled-worker quota only (400/country); no settlement rights and no recorded uptake.

excluded.name: AU-wide Free Movement Protocol
excluded.reason: Adopted 2018; insufficient ratifications to take legal effect continent-wide.

excluded.name: Schengen Area
excluded.reason: Travel/border arrangement, not a settlement-rights regime (settlement rights come from EU/EEA).

excluded.name: India-Sri Lanka
excluded.reason: 1954 Indo-Lanka Agreement covers only a registered legacy population (Indian-origin Tamils), not general citizens. 2024 visa-free entry for Indians is 30-day tourism, not settlement.

excluded.name: Svalbard Treaty open access
excluded.reason: Treaty nationals may live/work in Svalbard indefinitely, but it confers zero rights in mainland Norway - no settlement path, so out of scope.

excluded.name: San Marino-Italy
excluded.reason: De facto border openness confirmed, but the full reciprocal settlement-rights package could not be verified from primary sources.

excluded.name: Turkey-Northern Cyprus
excluded.reason: No clear primary legal source showing a formal privileged settlement lane on the Turkish side.

excluded.name: Portugal Sephardic naturalization
excluded.reason: Route narrowed/closed to new applicants - no longer operative as a live fast lane.

excluded.name: Netherlands-Suriname (post-colonial preference)
excluded.reason: The 1975 Toescheidingsovereenkomst's free-settlement transition ended in 1980; today only case-by-case option/reacquisition procedures remain - no general privileged lane into the Netherlands.

excluded.name: Belgium-DR Congo/Rwanda/Burundi
excluded.reason: No privileged residence or settlement regime for nationals of Belgium's former colonies - they are ordinary third-country nationals under Belgian law.

excluded.name: Italy-Libya/Somalia/Eritrea/Ethiopia
excluded.reason: Italy renounced colonial claims in 1947 and maintains no colonial-preference immigration lane; Italy's real fast lane is jure sanguinis descent citizenship.

excluded.name: South Africa ZEP/LEP permits
excluded.reason: Zimbabwean and Lesotho Exemption Permits (extended to May 2027) allow live/work/study but explicitly BAR permanent residency and status changes - temporary by design, out of settlement scope.

excluded.name: Commonwealth of Nations membership
excluded.reason: No general UK settlement rights from membership alone. Live UK routes that use Commonwealth status (e.g. Ancestry, some Youth Mobility partners) are separate bilateral lanes.

blocs.name: EU / EEA (+ Switzerland)

rights.TR: National-level only; no bloc-wide rights from a first-country TR.
rights.PR: EU Long-Term Residence Directive: after 5 years in one member state, limited conditional mobility to a second (work/study). Much weaker than citizen rights.
rights.CIT: Full right to live, work, study, and access healthcare/social security in every member state. Strongest bloc in the world.

blocs.fastest_entry: Cyprus ~4 yrs (B1 Greek + qualifying employment); standard 5 yrs in France, Belgium, Netherlands, Ireland, Luxembourg. Portugal now 10 yrs, Sweden/Finland 8.
blocs.notes: Switzerland linked via EU-Swiss free movement agreement rather than EEA membership. French Outermost Regions are EU territory: Guadeloupe, Martinique, French Guiana, Reunion, and Mayotte render inside the France shape; Saint-Martin has its own map feature and is listed explicitly. St-Barthelemy left the EU in 2012 (now an OCT), and the Dutch Caribbean (Aruba/Curacao/Sint Maarten) are OCTs outside the EU - their people are EU citizens via Dutch nationality, but EU law does not apply there.

blocs.name: Mercosur Residence Agreement

rights.TR: Any member-state citizen can obtain residency in another member on nationality alone (clean criminal record required).
rights.PR: Temporary residency converts to permanent after continuous legal residence (commonly ~2 yrs).
rights.CIT: Citizenship of any member = nationality-based residency access to all others. Naturalization timelines vary by country.

blocs.fastest_entry: Argentina: 2 yrs continuous residence (strict continuity per Decree 366/2025); investor CIT track pending as of 2026.
blocs.notes: Venezuela suspended. World's largest bloc by land area.

blocs.name: Andean Community (CAN)

rights.TR: Nationality-based residency rights similar to Mercosur.
rights.PR: Converts from TR after continuous residence.
rights.CIT: All four members also participate in the Mercosur Residence Agreement, so CAN citizenship effectively stacks both.

blocs.fastest_entry: Bolivia or Ecuador: 3 yrs. Peru in flux (2 yrs -> 5 yrs pending regulations).
blocs.notes: Colombia has signaled possible exit toward full Mercosur membership; unresolved as of mid-2026.

blocs.name: Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU)

rights.TR: Free movement of labor, mutual recognition of qualifications for member-state citizens.
rights.PR: National-level status; labor mobility is the main bloc benefit.
rights.CIT: Citizenship of any member = work rights across all five.

blocs.fastest_entry: Armenia: 3 yrs holding a residence permit, no minimum physical presence, dual citizenship allowed.
blocs.notes: Russia's renunciation requirement was eliminated in 2020; the 2023 law (138-FZ) requires only an unverified declaration of intent. Armenia remains the practical EAEU entry point for other reasons: no physical-presence requirement and a stable dual-citizenship regime.

blocs.name: Union State (Russia-Belarus)

rights.TR: Nationality-based: citizens of either state may reside in the other without normal immigration procedures (1999 treaty, in force 2000).
rights.PR: Permanent residence permit granted on the basis of citizenship alone; document review reduced to 2 months.
rights.CIT: Full mutual rights to live, work, settle, and own property. Belarusians explicitly exempt from Russia's 2025-26 naturalization restrictions.

blocs.fastest_entry: Belarus: ~7 yrs standard naturalization. Russia: no renunciation required since 2020 (138-FZ declaration only), but naturalized citizens face broad revocation grounds and Belarusians get the simplest track.
blocs.notes: Both members also in EAEU - Union State stacks deeper settlement rights on top of EAEU labor mobility.

blocs.name: Trans-Tasman Travel Arrangement

rights.TR: Not applicable; rights attach to citizenship.
rights.PR: Not applicable to third-country PRs of Australia (NZ extends some rights to Australian PRs).
rights.CIT: Citizens of either country can enter, live, and work indefinitely in the other (Special Category Visa for NZers in Australia).

blocs.fastest_entry: Australia: 4 yrs residence (1 as PR). NZ: 5 yrs.
blocs.notes: Direct NZ-citizen path to Australian citizenship added in 2023.

blocs.name: Common Travel Area (CTA)

rights.TR: Not applicable; rights attach to citizenship.
rights.PR: Not bloc-portable.
rights.CIT: Full mutual settlement rights between Irish and British citizens across all five jurisdictions. Predates and survives Brexit.

blocs.fastest_entry: Ireland: 5 yrs reckonable residence (vs UK 5+1). Irish passport stacks CTA + EU/EEA = broadest single-passport footprint (34-35 jurisdictions).

blocs.name: OECS (Eastern Caribbean)

rights.TR: Not the main mechanism.
rights.PR: Not the main mechanism.
rights.CIT: Citizenship acquired ANY way (incl. CBI) grants automatic full settlement rights across all 7 jurisdictions: work, business, healthcare, education, social security.

blocs.fastest_entry: Caribbean CBI: ~$200-250K donation, 6-12 months processing. Fastest bloc access in the world.
blocs.notes: 5 of 6 sovereign members run active CBI programs (SVG planned).

blocs.name: CARICOM Single Market (CSME)

rights.TR: Skills Certificate holders (12 approved worker categories) can work indefinitely without a permit. Not a general residency right.
rights.PR: No general bloc-level PR portability.
rights.CIT: Citizenship + Skills Certificate = indefinite work rights. CBI passports explicitly excluded from full CSME rights.

blocs.fastest_entry: Standard naturalization ~5 yrs in most members, then Skills Certificate.
blocs.notes: Oct 2025 'Enhanced Cooperation': Barbados, Belize, Dominica, SVG now give each other's citizens full free movement without a Skills Certificate.

blocs.name: East African Community (EAC)

rights.TR: Visa-free travel + renewable residence permits for member citizens; labor mobility restricted to high-skilled categories.
rights.PR: Settlement requires minimal paperwork for member citizens rather than discretionary grant.
rights.CIT: Citizenship of any member = bloc travel/residence rights.

blocs.fastest_entry: Rwanda: 5 yrs, dual citizenship allowed, active investor path. (Tanzania/Burundi 10 yrs, Uganda 20, DRC 5 but bans dual.)
blocs.notes: Implementation uneven in practice.

blocs.name: Nordic Passport Union (NPU)

rights.TR: Not bloc-portable.
rights.PR: Not bloc-portable.
rights.CIT: Free movement/settlement among Nordic citizens. Main added value beyond EU/EEA: Greenland + Faroe Islands (outside the EU).

blocs.fastest_entry: Iceland: 7 yrs (Sweden/Finland now 8, Norway 8, Denmark 9).
blocs.notes: Predates the EU; all 5 states are also EU or EEA members.

blocs.name: ECOWAS (West Africa)

rights.TR: Visa-free entry (90 days) + implemented right of residency via ECOWAS residence card.
rights.PR: Right of establishment (phase 3) unevenly implemented.
rights.CIT: Citizenship of any member = entry + residency rights bloc-wide.

blocs.fastest_entry: Naturalization set by each state individually; no bloc fast-track.
blocs.notes: Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger withdrew effective Jan 29, 2025 (now Alliance of Sahel States). ECOWAS still honors their citizens' movement/residence rights 'until further notice'.

blocs.name: Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC)

rights.TR: None bloc-portable for foreign residents (they are the majority population in most members).
rights.PR: None bloc-portable for foreigners.
rights.CIT: Full free movement, residence, work (public + private sector), healthcare, education, real estate, social insurance across all six.

blocs.fastest_entry: Effectively closed: Bahrain 25 yrs, Oman 20; Kuwait/Saudi/Qatar among world's most restrictive; UAE 'Nomination' track is discretionary and rare.
blocs.notes: Strong rights for existing citizens, essentially no entry door.

blocs.name: Compact of Free Association (CoFA)

rights.TR: n/a
rights.PR: n/a
rights.CIT: Birth citizens of FSM/RMI/Palau (and former Trust Territory citizens from the compact effective dates: 1986 FSM/RMI, 1994 Palau) may live/work/study in the US indefinitely as nonimmigrants. Americans may settle in all three Pacific states.

blocs.fastest_entry: Naturalized islanders qualify only after ~5 yrs actual residence post-naturalization; dual nationals can lose the privilege. Practical entry = US naturalization itself (5 yrs PR, 3 if married to a US citizen).
blocs.notes: US citizenship carries worldwide taxation.

blocs.name: India-Nepal Treaty (1950)

rights.TR: n/a
rights.PR: n/a
rights.CIT: Citizens of either country: full rights to travel, settle, work, and own property in the other, no visa. One of the world's most open bilateral arrangements.

blocs.fastest_entry: India: 12 yrs residence. Nepal: 15 yrs. No investment fast-track in either.
blocs.notes: Open border for citizens; very slow door for outsiders.

blocs.name: India-Bhutan Treaty (1949)

rights.TR: n/a
rights.PR: n/a
rights.CIT: Mutual visa-free entry, residence, and employment with no stay limits. Indians working in Bhutan access public healthcare. Mirrors the India-Nepal arrangement.

blocs.fastest_entry: Effectively closed: India 12 yrs residence; Bhutan naturalization 15-20 yrs, discretionary, among the world's most restrictive.
blocs.notes: India confirms only Nepal and Bhutan citizens are fully visa-exempt for indefinite live/work.

blocs.name: CPLP (Portuguese-speaking countries)

rights.TR: CPLP Mobility Agreement: real, operative visa/residence categories between members. Brazil grants residency to CPLP nationals on nationality alone (since Oct 2023). Portugal now requires a consular residence visa first (2025 law).
rights.PR: Portugal: CPLP permit 2 yrs -> renewable -> PR at 5 yrs.
rights.CIT: Portugal fast-track: 7 yrs to citizenship for CPLP nationals vs 10 for others.

blocs.fastest_entry: Hold any CPLP citizenship -> facilitated residency in Brazil and Portugal.
blocs.notes: Hub-and-spoke, not symmetric, and only PARTIALLY implemented: the Mobility Agreement is a framework domesticated state by state. Brazil and Portugal are operational; rights are not delivered identically across all nine states. Portugal-Brazil link is strongest (separate 1991/2005 Equal Rights treaty).

blocs.name: UK-Hong Kong BN(O) Route

rights.TR: BN(O) visa: 5-year UK residence permission to live, work, and study for BN(O) status holders and dependents.
rights.PR: ILR (settlement) after 5 continuous years on the route (B1 English + Life in the UK test).
rights.CIT: British citizenship 1 year after ILR ('5+1'). Confirmed exempt from the proposed 10-year baseline (Nov 2025). Direction: HK to UK only.

blocs.fastest_entry: Requires BN(O) status: HK residents from before the 1 Jul 1997 handover; adult children of holders newly eligible from 9 Feb 2026.
blocs.notes: One-way. BN(O) -> full British citizen also stacks CTA rights. Not available to Hong Kongers without BN(O) status.

blocs.name: UK & Overseas Territories

members.name: St. Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha

rights.TR: n/a - rights attach to citizenship status.
rights.PR: Each territory runs its own immigration control; British citizens get limited leave to remain, not settlement (e.g. Cayman PR is points/investment-based).
rights.CIT: All BOTCs became full British citizens on 21 May 2002 (except Cyprus SBAs): UK right of abode + CTA rights. Territory-to-UK direction is fully open; UK-to-territory is gated.

blocs.fastest_entry: Naturalize in a territory -> BOTC -> registrable as a British citizen. Slow but real (e.g. Cayman: 8 yrs to PR, then BOTC naturalization).
blocs.notes: One-way toward the UK. Montserrat is also an OECS member - the only territory in two blocs.

blocs.name: Cross-Strait & SAR Permits (Greater China)

rights.TR: HK/Macau/Taiwan permanent residents (Chinese citizens) may enter and reside in the mainland freely (Home Return Permit / Mainland Travel Permit).
rights.PR: Residence Permit for HK/Macau/Taiwan Residents (since Sep 2018) after 6 months residing in the mainland with work, residence, or study. Taiwan Settlement Permit converts to mainland household registration (hukou).
rights.CIT: Indefinite mainland residence for any purpose. Reverse direction strictly gated: mainland-to-HK/Macau settlement is a Mainland-administered quota system (One-Way Permit, 150/day incl. 60 for right-of-abode-by-descent and 30 for long-separated spouses) limited to family-reunion classes per the NIA's June 2026 notice (spouses separated 3+ yrs, minor children, elderly-care cases). Taiwan runs separate, security-screened residence-then-settlement tracks for HK/Macau residents and Mainland spouses.

blocs.fastest_entry: n/a for outsiders - rights attach to Chinese citizenship plus permanent residency of HK, Macau, or Taiwan.
blocs.notes: Asymmetric toward the mainland; permit mechanics only. Context: HK and Macau PR via 7 years' ordinary residence is ORDINARY immigration law open to anyone - it is not a privileged lane and is deliberately not mapped as an arrangement here.

blocs.name: Kingdom of the Netherlands

rights.TR: n/a - one shared Dutch nationality across all four Kingdom countries.
rights.PR: European Dutch settling in the Caribbean countries get near-automatic admission (good conduct + housing + means; no work permit needed in Curacao/Sint Maarten/BES). Aruba administers its own DIMAS admission.
rights.CIT: All Kingdom citizens hold DUTCH (EU) nationality: Caribbean-to-European-Netherlands direction is fully open (right of abode). Naturalizing in Curacao/Aruba/Sint Maarten yields the same Dutch/EU passport.

blocs.fastest_entry: Naturalize in any Kingdom country (5 yrs) -> Dutch nationality -> EU/EEA + the whole Kingdom. Caribbean naturalization is a real EU-passport door.
blocs.notes: Asymmetric but softer than the UK-BOT model: right of abode in the Caribbean countries is limited to those with a connection to the region, yet admission for other Dutch nationals is near-automatic with mild conditions. Note: Aruba, Curacao, and Sint Maarten are EU 'Overseas Countries & Territories' - OUTSIDE the EU (no EU law, no Schengen), even though their citizens hold EU citizenship via Dutch nationality. The French half of the island (Saint-Martin) IS in the EU as an Outermost Region.

blocs.name: ASEAN (proto)

rights.TR: None bloc-level.
rights.PR: None bloc-level.
rights.CIT: No residency/settlement rights. Only Mutual Recognition Agreements for ~8 licensed professions (~1.5% of workforce).

blocs.fastest_entry: n/a - no bloc settlement rights exist.
blocs.notes: The operative instrument (Agreement on the Movement of Natural Persons) covers TEMPORARY entry/stay only, for business visitors, intra-corporate transferees, and contractual service suppliers. Explicitly excludes citizenship/residence/permanent employment.

blocs.name: Central America Four (CA-4)

rights.TR: Passport-free travel between the four.
rights.PR: No formal settlement rights.
rights.CIT: Travel freedom only.

blocs.fastest_entry: n/a for settlement purposes.

blocs.name: Pacific Alliance (proto)

rights.TR: Visa-free mobility up to 6 months for UNPAID tourism, transit, and business activity only.
rights.PR: Not full freedom of movement; work and youth-mobility tracks still under development.
rights.CIT: Facilitation, not rights.

blocs.fastest_entry: Chile: ~5 yrs naturalization stacks Pacific Alliance facilitation + Mercosur Residence Agreement.
blocs.notes: The Alliance's own mobility page confirms the practical gain is travel facilitation, not a settlement regime.

bilateral_lanes.name: TN (USMCA professionals)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Canadian and Mexican citizens in ~60 listed professions: US work status, renewable indefinitely in 3-yr increments, no annual cap, no lottery. Canadians can apply at the border.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Nonimmigrant intent required - renewals can be denied if settlement intent is suspected. Employer-specific.

bilateral_lanes.name: E-3 (Australians)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Australian citizens: 10,500 dedicated US specialty-occupation visas per year (cap never reached), 2-yr renewable indefinitely, spouse may work.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Nonimmigrant status - does not itself lead to a green card.

bilateral_lanes.name: H-1B1 (Chile & Singapore)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Carve-out from the H-1B lottery: 1,400 visas/yr for Chileans, 5,400 for Singaporeans, rarely exhausted. Specialty occupations.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Nonimmigrant intent; 1-yr increments; no direct settlement path.

bilateral_lanes.name: E-2 Treaty Investor
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Full official State Dept treaty list (78 active E-2 countries painted). Notable absences: India, China (PRC), Brazil, Russia, Vietnam, Saudi Arabia. Bolivia and Ecuador are TERMINATED for new investors (grandfathered pre-2012/pre-2018 investments only). Kosovo also qualifies but has no ISO numeric code, so it is not painted. Greece and Brunei are E-1 (trader) only. UK nationals must actually reside in British territory in Europe to qualify.
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizens of treaty countries who make a substantial US business investment: renewable investor status with no fixed cap, spouse may work. Recent additions: Israel (2019), New Zealand (2019), Portugal (Mar 2024).
bilateral_lanes.limits: Must maintain the investment; nonimmigrant status with no direct green card path; 'substantial' is undefined but typically $100K+.
sources: US State Dept treaty country list: travel.state.gov/content/travel/en/us-visas/visa-information-resources/fees/treaty.html

bilateral_lanes.name: Spain 2-year naturalization (Ibero-American)
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: All Ibero-American nationals (incl. Brazil) + Philippines, Andorra, Equatorial Guinea, Portugal
bilateral_lanes.grants: Naturalization after 2 years of legal residence (vs 10 standard), dual citizenship permitted. Result: full EU/EEA citizenship. Requires DELE A2 Spanish + civics test; processing backlogs add 1-2+ yrs.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Residence must be legal and continuous immediately before applying.

bilateral_lanes.name: UK Youth Mobility (expanded AU/CA/NZ tier)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Ages 18-35 (vs 18-30 standard), 3 years (vs 2), no ballot/cap for AU-CA-NZ, work in almost any job, no sponsor. Reciprocal: UK citizens get Australia's Working Holiday to 35, 3 yrs, no farm-work requirement.
bilateral_lanes.limits: YMS time does NOT count toward Indefinite Leave to Remain; one use per lifetime; no dependants. This is the closest existing thing to 'CANZUK', which remains an advocacy proposal with no treaty.

bilateral_lanes.name: UK Ancestry visa
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Commonwealth citizens with a UK-born grandparent - descent-conditional, so beneficiary countries are not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: 5-year work visa, renewable, leads to Indefinite Leave to Remain then citizenship.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Needs a UK-born grandparent. Commonwealth citizenship alone is not enough.

bilateral_lanes.name: Taiwan Employment Gold Card
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Foreign specialists in designated fields - skill-based, not nationality-mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Open work + residence for up to 3 years (renewable), no sponsor. Path to permanent residence after about 3 years of qualifying stay.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Field eligibility is reviewed. Permanent residence only — not citizenship, and not Cross-Strait / HK / Macau rights.

bilateral_lanes.name: Argentina citizenship claim (Falklands-born)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Argentine law treats the islands as Argentine territory (jus soli): native-born islanders who declare themselves Argentine before Argentine authorities have been recognized as Argentine nationals BY BIRTH - unlocking Mercosur residence rights.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Legislation is inconclusive; recognition is case-by-case on individual declaration, politically charged, and take-up among islanders is essentially nil. Draft bills to formalize it have not passed.

bilateral_lanes.name: Dutch-American Friendship Treaty (DAFT)
bilateral_lanes.grants: US citizens: 2-yr renewable Dutch self-employment residence with only ~EUR 4,500 business investment - far below normal entrepreneur thresholds. Renewable toward PR at 5 yrs.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Must maintain the business and investment. Investment figure approximate.

bilateral_lanes.name: Panama Friendly Nations Visa
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: 50 'friendly nations' per Executive Decree 226 (2021). NOT removed, but tightened: the 2012 decree's instant PR became a 2-yr provisional first, with a qualifying tie required.
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizens of ~50 friendly nations: 2-yr provisional residency, then PERMANENT residency (no expiry), eventually naturalization (5 yrs). Qualify via Panamanian employment, USD 200K real estate, or a USD 200K 3-yr bank deposit. Approval ~4-6 months.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Decree 226 (Jul 2021) replaced the original 2012 instant-PR regime - a qualifying economic/professional tie is now mandatory. Government fees ~USD 1,050 + legal fees.
sources: Executive Decree 226 of July 2021 (replacing Decree 416 of 2012)

bilateral_lanes.name: Russia CIS work-patent regime
bilateral_lanes.grants: Visa-free CIS nationals outside the EAEU: enter Russia without a visa and work via a 'patent' (monthly tax prepayment, 1-12 months, renewable without leaving) - no quota, no employer sponsorship. EAEU citizens (Armenia/Belarus/Kazakhstan/Kyrgyzstan) need no patent at all - that's the EAEU bloc.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Work access only - the patent confers no residence-to-settlement progression by itself. Regional patents restrict where you may work. Ukraine's inclusion is war-era volatile; verify current practice before relying on it.
sources: Russian GUVM work-patent rules for visa-free CIS nationals

bilateral_lanes.name: Australia Pacific Engagement Visa
bilateral_lanes.grants: PERMANENT residence by annual ballot (subclass 192) for citizens of Pacific states + Timor-Leste. Citizenship follows Australia's ordinary residence rules. 2026-27 ballot open.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Ballot-allocated annual quota; eligible-country list is administratively maintained and can change.
sources: Australian Dept of Home Affairs: Pacific Engagement visa (subclass 192)

bilateral_lanes.name: NZ Samoan Quota
bilateral_lanes.grants: Permanent residence by annual ballot for Samoan citizens with a permanent full-time NZ job offer. Citizenship follows normal NZ grant rules.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Annual quota; requires winning the ballot + job offer.

bilateral_lanes.name: NZ Pacific Access Category
bilateral_lanes.grants: Permanent residence by annual ballot for citizens of Fiji, Kiribati, Tonga, Tuvalu with a permanent full-time NZ job offer.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Annual quota; ballot + job offer required.
sources: Immigration New Zealand: Pacific Access Category

bilateral_lanes.name: NZ Realm citizenship (Cook Is/Niue/Tokelau)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Cook Islanders, Niueans, and Tokelauans ARE New Zealand citizens - full right to enter and live in NZ. Sits at the nationality layer, not the immigration layer.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Constitutional linkage, deeply entrenched. NZ citizenship also unlocks Australia via the Trans-Tasman arrangement.
sources: NZ Dept of Internal Affairs: Citizenship guidance
Immigration NZ: Who are New Zealand citizens?

bilateral_lanes.name: France-Algeria 1968 Accord
bilateral_lanes.grants: Special residence/work regime for Algerian nationals in France, distinct from (and in several respects more favorable than) the ordinary immigration code - including work, family, and 10-year certificate tracks leading to settlement and naturalization.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Heavily contested in French politics and repeatedly threatened in 2025 - still in force but among the most fragile major fast lanes in Europe.

bilateral_lanes.name: Monaco-France free establishment
bilateral_lanes.grants: Monegasque nationals may live and work in France with no residence or work permit - long-settled special bilateral position.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Applies to Monaco's ~9,000 citizens only (most Monaco residents are not Monegasque nationals).
sources: Embassy of Monaco in France: Living in France

bilateral_lanes.name: Japan EPA nurse/careworker lanes
bilateral_lanes.grants: Treaty-based candidate entry into Japan's care system; after passing Japanese licensure, workers may stay and work indefinitely as licensed professionals with no renewal-count limits - a real long-term settlement funnel.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Profession-specific (nursing/care work) via designated public matching; candidate period requires passing the national exam. Japan bans dual citizenship at naturalization.
sources: Japan MHLW: Acceptance of foreign nurse and careworker candidates
Japan MOFA: Long-term stay visa guide

bilateral_lanes.name: France-Morocco 1987 Accord
bilateral_lanes.grants: Nationality-specific stay-and-employment regime displacing ordinary French immigration law on covered points; supports residence-card progression toward settlement.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Not open migration - work-based admission under the accord's rules; French law applies where the treaty is silent.
sources: CAA Toulouse 2023 decision applying the accord

bilateral_lanes.name: France-Tunisia 1988 Accord
bilateral_lanes.grants: Treaty-based work and family admission with a nationality-specific residence regime (1988 accord + 2000 avenant + 2008 migration protocol), leading to settlement.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Applies on covered points only; French general law fills gaps.

bilateral_lanes.name: France-Senegal conventions package
bilateral_lanes.grants: 1995 circulation/stay convention + 2000 establishment convention + 2006/2008 'gestion concertee' package: derogatory work/stay channels supporting residence progression to settlement.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Channel-specific rather than general free movement.

bilateral_lanes.name: France-Gabon conventions package
bilateral_lanes.grants: 1992 circulation/stay + 2002 establishment conventions: nationals of each state may enter, travel, choose residence, and depart under the conventions' conditions; 2007 accord supplements.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Convention conditions apply; unusually well documented in treaty publication and case law.

bilateral_lanes.name: Andorra mobility conventions (ES/FR/PT)
bilateral_lanes.grants: Privileged entry, movement, stay, and establishment in Andorra for Spanish, French (2001 convention), and Portuguese (2007 convention) nationals - cited in current Andorran e-government residence procedures.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Residence/work privilege only: Andorran naturalization stays ordinary (20 yrs standard, 10 with schooling) and Andorra bans dual citizenship.

bilateral_lanes.name: Israel Law of Return
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Jews and eligible family members worldwide (incl. the 1970 grandchild clause) - identity-based, not nationality-based, so beneficiary countries are not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Immigration-and-citizenship entitlement (aliyah): moves a qualifying outsider straight into a citizenship track with minimal prior residence. One of the most powerful settlement lanes in the world.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Grandchild clause is politically contested (2025 narrowing bill failed but pressure continues) - current breadth is not guaranteed forever.

bilateral_lanes.name: Poland Karta Polaka
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: People of Polish descent/nation affiliation (historically concentrated in post-Soviet states) - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Pole's Card -> national visa -> permanent residence -> recognition as citizen after ~1 yr of residence with PR. Bundles diaspora identity with hard migration outcomes; dual citizenship OK.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Must prove Polish affiliation/descent; subject to ongoing legislative tinkering.
sources: gov.pl: Recognition as a Polish citizen

bilateral_lanes.name: Hungary simplified naturalization
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: People with Hungarian ancestry or former Hungarian citizenship (concentrated in neighboring states) - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizenship WITHOUT residence in Hungary: prove ancestry + Hungarian language ability. EU citizenship as the result. Operating since 2011.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Language requirement is real; politically foundational so unlikely to narrow, but scrutiny of applications varies.
sources: Embassy of Hungary: About Hungarian Citizenship

bilateral_lanes.name: Germany Spataussiedler route
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Ethnic German resettlers from the former Soviet bloc under the Federal Expellees Act - ancestry-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Recognition as a late ethnic German resettler confers German nationality automatically upon entry - ethnic eligibility, managed admission, automatic citizenship.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Statutory eligibility cohorts are limited (mainly post-Soviet ethnic Germans); documentation requirements substantial.

bilateral_lanes.name: Kazakhstan Qandas status
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Ethnic Kazakhs abroad - ethnicity-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Ethnic Kazakhs entering Kazakhstan receive Qandas status with linked residence-permit channels, then SIMPLIFIED citizenship regardless of length of residence - one of the clearest ethnic-return fast lanes anywhere (2026 digital pilot links PR issuance to status services).
bilateral_lanes.limits: Kazakhstan bans dual citizenship. Active policy area with periodic administrative redesign.
sources: eGov.kz: Status and rights of kandas (2026 pilot)

bilateral_lanes.name: Russia Compatriot Resettlement
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: 'Compatriots abroad' (broad cultural/historical definition) - identity-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Federal programme: qualify as a compatriot, join via a participating region, relocate, and use an accelerated settlement/citizenship track. Dual citizenship tolerated.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Embedded in a volatile wider migration environment; region-linked obligations apply.
sources: Kremlin: State Programme for voluntary resettlement of compatriots

bilateral_lanes.name: South Korea F-4 Overseas Korean
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Overseas Koreans (ethnic Koreans with foreign nationality) - diaspora-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: F-4 visa: broad labor-market access + credible downline to F-5 permanent residence. Functions as a quasi-membership lane for the Korean diaspora.
bilateral_lanes.limits: PR/citizenship require ordinary residence; Korea restricts dual citizenship for naturalizers (conditional exceptions).
sources: Korea Visa Portal: F-4 Overseas Korean

bilateral_lanes.name: Japan Nikkei Long-Term Resident
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Nikkei (Japanese descendants, mainly in Brazil/Peru) through 4th generation - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Long-Term Resident status for qualifying Japanese descendants; PR and naturalization available after ordinary residence.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Japan has repeatedly adjusted descendant subcategories; bans dual citizenship at naturalization.
sources: Japan MOJ: Long-Term Resident for Nikkei descendants

bilateral_lanes.name: India OCI (Overseas Citizen of India)
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Persons of Indian origin + eligible spouses worldwide - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Lifelong multiple-entry status with indefinite stay and no registration limits - stronger than a visa, weaker than nationality. Statutory path to citizenship after 5 yrs as OCI + ordinary residence (s.5(1)(g)).
bilateral_lanes.limits: India bans dual citizenship - taking Indian citizenship means renouncing the other. OCI benefits/compliance rules adjusted periodically.

bilateral_lanes.name: Ireland Foreign Births Register
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Anyone with an Irish-born grandparent - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizenship by descent via registration - no residence required, no language test. Result: EU/EEA + CTA, the broadest single-passport footprint. Dual citizenship OK.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Requires an Irish-born grandparent (or parent registered before your birth); processing takes 1-2 yrs.
sources: Irish Immigration Service: citizenship by birth or descent

bilateral_lanes.name: Italy jure sanguinis (post-2025)
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Italian descendants under the reworked post-2025 rules - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizenship by descent with no residence requirement if applying via consulate. Result: EU/EEA citizenship. Dual OK.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Law 74/2025 significantly TIGHTENED eligibility (generational limits); implementation still politically active - verify current rules before relying on this.
sources: Italian consular guidance: Cittadinanza iure sanguinis - nuova disciplina (Law 74/2025)

bilateral_lanes.name: Armenia ethnic-Armenian citizenship
bilateral_lanes.beneficiaries_note: Ethnic Armenians and descendants worldwide - descent-based, not mapped
bilateral_lanes.grants: Citizenship by descent/ethnicity with no general residence requirement (new e-system since Jan 2026). Armenian citizenship also carries EAEU work rights across 5 states.
bilateral_lanes.limits: Procedural; documentation of Armenian descent required. Dual citizenship OK.

stacking_plays.footprint: 34-35 jurisdictions — broadest single-passport footprint via naturalization
Redundant double coverage of South America

Brazil-to-EU pipeline: Brazilian citizenship (1 yr as parent of a Brazilian child) -> Spain's 2-yr Ibero-American naturalization -> full EU/EEA citizenship. Beats the 7-yr CPLP route to Portugal, which Brazilian citizenship also unlocks.
Uniquely dual-bloc by birth: British citizen (BOTC 2002) = UK + CTA, plus a claimable Argentine nationality-by-birth = Mercosur. Argentine recognition is case-by-case and politically charged.

pending_verification.name: Taiwan special residence/settlement regime for HK & Macau residents
pending_verification.reason: Distinct legal regime confirmed in primary law (Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Act), but post-2020 security-screening practice has shifted administratively rather than by clean codified amendment - residence-to-settlement timing imprecise.
sources: Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Act

pending_verification.name: Taiwan Mainland-spouse residence and settlement track
pending_verification.reason: Special spouse regime is real in primary law, but latest operational details are fragmented across Taiwan ministries; 2025 policy updates affected long-term-residence administration.

pending_verification.name: France-Cameroon circulation/stay package
pending_verification.reason: 1994 convention force is clear, but the practical reach of the 2009 'gestion concertee' accord is narrower and more administrative than the Maghreb/Senegal/Gabon packages.
sources: French court citation of the 2009 accord

pending_verification.name: France-Mali circulation and establishment package
pending_verification.reason: CESEDA annex confirms the 1994 conventions formally survive, but current administrative practice is politically exposed given the state of France-Mali relations.

pending_verification.name: Liechtenstein-Switzerland migration framework
pending_verification.reason: 2008 bilateral framework verified; Switzerland recognizes a 5-yr settlement effect for Liechtenstein nationals, but Liechtenstein retains quota/lottery limits for Swiss nationals - the edge beyond the EEA/Swiss baseline is asymmetric and technical. Moved here from the excluded list after batch-2 research.

pending_verification.name: Suriname Persons of Surinamese Origin law (2014)
pending_verification.reason: Suriname's 2014 PSA law grants Surinamese-origin persons (largely Dutch nationals of Surinamese descent) residence/work rights in Suriname - a diaspora status short of citizenship (no voting rights). Operative per 2025 legal commentary, but primary-law detail not yet verified to the live bar.

158.note: Taiwan (R.O.C.): naturalizers generally must produce a certificate of loss of original nationality within one year of permission (statutory exceptions for high-level professionals and special contribution).

356.note: India: no dual citizenship; OCI is a quasi-status, not nationality.

360.note: Indonesia: adult dual nationality is not retained on naturalization under Law No. 12 of 2006; limited dual for children ends at majority.

392.note: Japan: naturalization requires renouncing prior citizenship.

398.note: Kazakhstan: dual citizenship prohibited, incl. for kandas.

410.note: South Korea: naturalizers generally must renounce prior nationality; limited dual-nationality exceptions exist (e.g. marriage migrants, outstanding talent).

458.note: Malaysia: naturalizers must renounce prior nationality.

566.note: Nigeria: section 28 requires renunciation of other nationalities for naturalization/registration, except citizenship of a country acquired by birth.

643.note: Russia: renunciation requirement eliminated in 2020; 138-FZ (2023) requires only an unverified declaration of intent. Caveat: naturalized citizens face broad revocation grounds (138-FZ art. 22-24, expanded July 2025) - acquired citizenship is legally weaker than birth citizenship.

treaty_exceptions.name: Russia-Tajikistan Dual Citizenship Treaty (1995)
treaty_exceptions.effect: The sole surviving Russian dual-citizenship treaty: changes conflict-of-laws treatment (military service, consular protection, document recognition) for Russia-Tajikistan dual citizens. Not a mobility lane - and NOT what permits keeping both citizenships (general Russian law already allows that since 2020).

treaty_exceptions.name: Argentina-Spain Nationality Convention (1969, 2001 protocol)
treaty_exceptions.effect: Protected bilateral double-nationality framework once citizenship is otherwise acquired - not a right to move or settle automatically.

treaty_exceptions.name: Argentina-Italy Citizenship Agreement (1971)
treaty_exceptions.effect: Quiescent/full citizenship mechanics between the two states; Italy's 1992 general dual-citizenship law reduced its marginal effect, which is narrower than often claimed.

generational_events.child: Brazilian citizenship at birth (jus soli) - Mercosur + CPLP + Spain's 2-yr Ibero-American track from day one.
generational_events.parent: Parent naturalization after just 1 year of residence.
sources: Brazil Ministry of Justice naturalization materials

generational_events.child: Mexican citizenship at birth (jus soli) - TN professional access to the US at working age, E-2 investor eligibility, Spain 2-yr track.
generational_events.parent: Parent naturalization after 2 years of residence (vs 5 standard).

generational_events.child: Argentine citizenship at birth (jus soli) - Mercosur-wide residence rights.
generational_events.parent: Parent gets family-based permanent residence, then Argentina's ordinary 2-yr naturalization.
